text.title: ops-annual-planning-process Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for ops-annual-planning-process. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Cap annual priorities at exactly 3. If more than 3 priorities are proposed, refuse to proceed until one is dropped. Do not silently accept a 4th priority.
rule.rationale: More than 3 annual priorities dilutes focus to the point where nothing compounds. The constraint is the mechanism that makes the plan actionable. LLMs default to 7-item lists; the cap must be enforced in prompts.

rule.statement: Schedule all four review cadences (weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual) on the calendar before the year starts. Agent holds the slot but humans must show up; agent must not skip a human review even if metrics look fine.
rule.rationale: Cadence drift — monthly check-ins compressed to Slack messages, quarterly reviews skipped — is the primary cause of plans becoming irrelevant by Q2. Calendar commitment is the only reliable mechanism.

rule.statement: Every key result must have: a numeric target with unit and date, a current baseline, a measurable data source available at reporting time, and a kill-switch (when do we stop this initiative?) and a leading indicator (what changes first if we are wrong?).
rule.rationale: KRs without these fields cannot be scored objectively. "Improve customer experience meaningfully" is decoration. "Increase NPS from 32 to 45 by Q3, measured monthly via Delighted" is a KR.

rule.statement: Define the metric layer (which tool is the source of truth for each KR) at the start of the year. Lock definitions before Q1 starts and never silently change them mid-year.
rule.rationale: Goal scoring against shifting definitions is misleading. "Customer" in January must mean the same thing in December.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
